questions.question: What are the two primary entry types for journeys in Adobe Journey Optimizer?
options: Triggered Journeys and Scheduled Journeys
Unitary (event-triggered) Journeys and Audience-based (read segment) Journeys
Real-time Journeys and Batch Journeys
Inbound Journeys and Outbound Journeys
questions.explanation: AJO supports two primary journey entry types: Unitary journeys (triggered by a specific event for a single profile, like a purchase confirmation or an abandoned cart signal) and Audience-based journeys (trigger for all members of a segment at a scheduled time, like a weekly newsletter campaign or a monthly loyalty tier review). Each serves fundamentally different use cases: Unitary journeys are designed for real-time, 1:1 reactive experiences where timing matters (e.g., reacting within seconds of an action), while Audience-based journeys suit planned, broadcast-style communications to a defined cohort. A practical gotcha: Unitary journeys require that the triggering event schema be registered in AEP as an Experience Event and configured in AJO's Event catalog before the journey can reference it. Audience-based journeys depend on segment evaluation completing before the scheduled run time, so batch-evaluated segments should be given sufficient lead time to ensure all qualifying profiles are captured.

questions.question: In AJO, what is the purpose of the 'Wait' activity in a journey?
options: To pause the journey until a specific audience segment is refreshed with new members
To hold a profile at a point in the journey for a defined duration or until a specific date/time
To delay message delivery until the recipient's local timezone reaches business hours
To pause journey execution while an external API call completes its asynchronous processing
questions.explanation: The Wait activity in AJO holds an individual profile at that point in the journey for a configured duration (e.g., 2 hours, 3 days) or until a specific date/time. This is used to create time-delayed follow-ups (e.g., send a reminder email 3 days after initial purchase), pacing communications, and building time-based drip sequences. There are two Wait configuration modes: Duration-based (wait X hours/days/weeks relative to when the profile arrived) and Date/Time-based (wait until a fixed calendar date or a date stored in a profile attribute, such as a subscription expiry date). An important practical note: profiles held in a Wait node DO count against the journey's concurrent profile capacity, so very long waits on high-volume journeys require careful capacity planning. Wait nodes also interact with journey timeout settings — if the global journey timeout elapses while a profile is waiting, the profile exits the journey silently without proceeding to subsequent steps.

questions.question: What channel implementation is required to enable authoring and delivery of Web Channel campaigns in AJO?
options: A server-side rendering framework like Next.js with SSR enabled
Adobe Experience Platform Web SDK (Alloy.js) on the website - client-side only or hybrid mode
Adobe Analytics tags with the Journey Optimizer extension installed
A dedicated AJO mobile SDK installed on the web server
questions.explanation: Web Channel delivery requires Adobe Experience Platform Web SDK (`alloy.js`) on the website. Two implementation modes are supported: Client-side only (alloy.js handles both the personalization decision request and the DOM rendering) and Hybrid mode (AEP Edge Network Server API handles personalization decisions server-side, but alloy.js renders content client-side). Both modes require alloy.js for rendering because the visual modifications authored in the AJO web designer must execute in the browser context. A common implementation mistake is loading alloy.js asynchronously without the `renderDecisions: true` flag, which results in the personalization call being made but content never rendering. Teams migrating from Adobe Target should note that the Web SDK replaces `at.js` — they cannot coexist on the same page for web channel delivery. Additionally, if the target website enforces a strict Content Security Policy (CSP), exceptions for AJO's authoring domain must be added or the Visual Editing Helper extension will fail to load the page correctly.

questions.question: Which browser extension must be installed to author and preview web pages in the AJO web designer?
options: Adobe Experience Cloud Debugger extension
Adobe Experience Cloud Visual Editing Helper extension
Adobe Target VEC (Visual Experience Composer) Helper extension
Adobe Launch Browser Extension for Tag Management
questions.explanation: The Adobe Experience Cloud Visual Editing Helper browser extension is a prerequisite for the AJO web designer. Without it, you cannot reliably open, author, or preview web pages within the Journey Optimizer web designer interface. It enables the visual drag-and-drop editing capabilities on live web pages by injecting a communication bridge between the AJO authoring UI and the target web page loaded inside an iframe. A key practical detail: the extension must be enabled for the specific origin (domain) of the website being edited — if disabled for that domain, the designer will display the page but editing interactions will fail silently. The extension works only in Chrome and Edge browsers; Firefox is not supported for web authoring. If the target website has a Content Security Policy (CSP) that blocks iframes or inline scripts, the extension may not be able to load the page correctly, requiring CSP exceptions to be added for the AJO authoring domain.

questions.question: For web experience delivery to work correctly, what must be enabled in the AEP Data Collection datastream?
options: Adobe Analytics connector with report suite mapping configured
Adobe Journey Optimizer option under the Adobe Experience Platform service
Adobe Target extension with the VEC Helper enabled
Real-Time CDP integration with the web channel connector activated
questions.explanation: For web channel delivery, in Adobe Experience Platform Data Collection you must have a datastream configured with the 'Adobe Journey Optimizer' option enabled under the Adobe Experience Platform service. Without this, personalization decisions from AJO cannot be delivered through the Edge Network to the Web SDK — the Edge Network will process events but will not include AJO propositions in its response. A practical gotcha: enabling the AJO option in the datastream is separate from enabling Adobe Analytics or Adobe Target in the same datastream; you may have all three enabled simultaneously on a single datastream. The datastream must also be referenced correctly in your alloy.js `configure` call via the `datastreamId` property — using the wrong datastream (e.g., a dev datastream in production) is a frequent source of web channel delivery issues. When using Adobe Tags (Launch) for alloy.js deployment, ensure the correct datastream is selected in the AEP Web SDK extension configuration for each environment (dev, staging, prod) independently.

questions.question: What is 'Hybrid Mode' for the AJO Web Channel implementation?
options: A mode that supports both desktop and mobile web experiences in one campaign
Server-side API requests for personalization decisions combined with client-side alloy.js rendering
A combination of AJO campaigns and Journey activities targeting the same web page
A mode that uses both A/B testing and rule-based personalization simultaneously
questions.explanation: Hybrid mode uses the AEP Edge Network Server API to request personalization decisions server-side (for benefits like SEO-friendly rendering, reduced client-side latency, and the ability to cache decisions at the CDN edge), while alloy.js on the client side renders the personalized modifications returned in the server response. The key technical flow is: (1) the server makes an authenticated call to the Edge Network Server API including the visitor's identity, (2) the Edge Network returns proposition JSON containing the content modifications, (3) that proposition JSON is embedded in the HTML response sent to the browser, (4) alloy.js reads the embedded propositions and applies the DOM modifications. A critical gotcha with hybrid mode is identity stitching — the server-side call must pass a valid ECID that matches what alloy.js uses client-side, otherwise the personalization context is split across two anonymous identities. Hybrid mode is the recommended approach for SSR frameworks like Next.js or Nuxt where client-only rendering creates a flash of unmodified content (FOUC) before personalization loads.

questions.question: In AJO, what is a 'Content Fragment' and what is it primarily used for?
options: A saved filter rule that can be reused across multiple audience segment definitions
A reusable content block that can be referenced in multiple emails across campaigns and journeys
A pre-built journey template that accelerates the creation of common workflow patterns
A cached version of dynamic content that is pre-rendered for faster email delivery
questions.explanation: Content Fragments in AJO are reusable content blocks (building blocks) that can be referenced in one or more emails across multiple campaigns and journeys. They enable marketers to pre-build brand-aligned content components (headers, footers, product blocks, legal disclaimers) that are maintained in one place and automatically updated everywhere they are used — the 'single source of truth' principle applied to email content. If a legal team updates a disclaimer text in the Content Fragment, every email referencing that fragment will automatically include the updated text without requiring individual campaign edits. Content Fragments are authored using the same Email Designer experience as full emails, so they support the same HTML, layout, images, and personalization expressions. An important distinction: fragments are embedded by reference at authoring time — modifying a fragment after a campaign is published affects future sends but not already-delivered messages. Fragments can also include personalization expressions, so a reusable product block fragment can still render individualized content for each recipient.

questions.question: What is the AJO API endpoint for creating a content fragment programmatically?
options: POST /ajo/messages/templates
POST /ajo/content/fragments
POST /ajo/assets/reusable-blocks
POST /ajo/design/components
questions.explanation: The AJO API endpoint for programmatically creating content fragments is `POST /ajo/content/fragments`. This enables marketers and developers to automate the creation of personalized content components, streamlining the process of building dynamic email headers, banners, and other reusable content blocks at scale. Using the API is particularly valuable in enterprise workflows where content is generated or approved in external systems (like a DAM or CMS) and needs to be synchronized into AJO without manual copy-paste. The request body must include the fragment's name, status, channel type, and the `content` object containing the HTML/JSON representation. The API also supports `GET /ajo/content/fragments` for listing, `GET /ajo/content/fragments/{fragmentId}` for retrieval, `PATCH` for updates, and `DELETE` for removal. Authentication uses Bearer tokens obtained through the Adobe IMS OAuth flow with the appropriate `ajo.write` scope. A practical tip: when automating fragment creation, always check for existing fragments with the same name before creating a new one to avoid duplicates that confuse marketers in the designer UI.

questions.question: What is the difference between a 'Content Fragment' and a 'Content Template' in AJO?
options: Fragments are for images; Templates are for text-based content blocks
Fragments are reusable content BLOCKS embedded within messages; Templates are full content FRAMEWORKS for entire messages
Fragments are used in Journeys; Templates are used in Campaigns only
Fragments are created by admins; Templates can be created by any marketing user
questions.explanation: In AJO: Content Fragments are smaller reusable BUILDING BLOCKS (like a header, footer, or promotional banner component) embedded within a larger message. Content Templates are full content FRAMEWORKS for an entire message — a complete email layout (structure, branding, placeholder sections) that can be reused and adapted for individual campaigns. Templates = whole message structure; Fragments = reusable components within a message. A practical way to visualize this layering: a Content Template might define a two-column email layout with a branded header, body section placeholders, and a standard footer — while the branded header and footer themselves are Content Fragments embedded inside that template. When a marketer creates a new email campaign, they select the Content Template as a starting point and customize only the campaign-specific sections. This layered approach allows brand governance teams to lock down structural and legal elements (via Fragments) while giving campaign teams creative freedom in variable content zones. Both Fragments and Templates support preview with test profiles in the AJO designer.

questions.question: In the AJO Email Designer Code Editor, which syntax is used for personalization expressions?
options: JSX/React component syntax: <PersonalizationTag field='profile.firstName' />
Double curly brace Handlebars syntax: {{profile.person.name.firstName}}
Liquid template syntax: {{ customer.first_name }}
Jinja2 template syntax: {{ profile['firstName'] }}
questions.explanation: AJO uses an inline simple personalization syntax based on Handlebars with double curly braces: `{{profile.person.name.firstName}}`. This syntax can be used anywhere in the email content — subject lines, preview text, body HTML, button labels, image alt attributes — to inject profile attributes, contextual data, and helper function results. Multiple expressions can be combined: `Hello {{profile.person.name.firstName}}, your order #{{context.journey.orderNumber}} is confirmed.` The personalization engine evaluates these expressions at send time using the profile's most recent Real-Time Customer Profile snapshot. A common gotcha: expressions referencing nested XDM fields must follow the exact XDM schema path — using `{{profile.firstName}}` instead of `{{profile.person.name.firstName}}` produces an empty string silently rather than an error, which can result in blank personalization in production emails. Always validate expressions using the Preview function with test profiles before publishing. Custom attributes stored under a tenant namespace must include the `_tenantId` prefix: `{{profile._yourTenantId.customMixin.fieldName}}`.

questions.question: What are the four main channels available for delivering messages in Adobe Journey Optimizer?
options: Email, WhatsApp, LinkedIn, and Direct Mail
Email, Push Notifications, SMS/MMS, and In-App Messaging
Email, SMS, Twitter Direct Messages, and Web Push
Email, Push Notifications, SMS, and Fax
questions.explanation: AJO's four primary message delivery channels are: Email (transactional, marketing, and operational — supporting HTML, AMP for Email, and plain text), Push Notifications (iOS/Android app notifications delivered via APNs and FCM respectively), SMS/MMS (text messages via certified SMS gateway providers like Sinch, Twilio, and Infobip), and In-App Messaging (rich messages displayed within mobile apps while the user is actively using the app). Beyond these four core channels, AJO also supports Web channel (browser-based personalization via alloy.js), Direct Mail (physical print/postal integration via third-party providers), and Custom Actions (for any channel reachable via REST API, such as WhatsApp Business API, Slack, or a proprietary push system). Each channel requires its own channel surface (formerly called a 'preset') configured by an administrator, defining technical settings like the sending domain for email, the FCM/APNs credentials for push, or the SMS provider API keys. Channel surfaces also enforce governance settings like unsubscribe handling and suppression list integration.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an 'API-Triggered Campaign' in AJO?
options: A campaign that uses the REST API to build audience segments before activation
A high-priority communication (like password resets) triggered by an external system via API call
A campaign that triggers when a user calls the brand's customer service API
A scheduled campaign that is activated through the AJO admin API
questions.explanation: API-Triggered Campaigns in AJO are for transactional, high-priority communications (password resets, order confirmations, shipping updates, OTP codes) where an external system initiates the message via an API call. The external system generates unique contextual values (like a reset URL or a tracking number) and passes them in the JSON payload of the API request; AJO injects these values into the message template and sends immediately. Unlike scheduled campaigns (which run at a fixed time to a pre-defined segment), API-triggered campaigns are real-time and individual — one API call triggers one message to one specific recipient. The triggering endpoint is `POST /campaigns/{campaignId}/triggers` and requires authentication via an IMS Bearer token. A key design principle: the campaign message template is authored and approved in AJO in advance, while the API payload only supplies dynamic contextual values at send time — this separation of template governance from runtime data keeps communications brand-consistent and operationally flexible. The calling system should implement retry logic since AJO returns a `202 Accepted` response (not a delivery confirmation), and actual delivery status is available via reporting or feedback events.

questions.question: In AJO's journey canvas, what does a 'Condition' activity do?
options: It applies a data governance policy to prevent sending to restricted profiles
It evaluates a rule and routes profiles to different paths based on whether the condition is true or false
It pauses the journey and waits until an external system confirms a condition is met
It filters incoming events to determine which ones trigger the journey
questions.explanation: The Condition activity in AJO's journey canvas evaluates a defined rule for each profile and routes them to different journey paths based on the outcome (true/false branches, or up to multiple named branches). For example: 'If loyalty tier = Gold → send premium offer email; else → send standard offer email.' This is the fundamental branching mechanism in journey design, enabling true 1:1 personalization of the journey path itself — not just the message content. There are three types of Condition activities: Data Condition (evaluates profile attributes or contextual data), Event Condition (checks whether a specific event occurred within a time window — e.g., 'did they open the email within 24 hours?'), and Time Condition (routes based on the day of week or time of day the profile reached that step). Conditions support 'Advanced Mode' which exposes the full expression editor, allowing complex multi-field logic using AND/OR operators and helper functions. Always connect the 'Otherwise' branch to avoid profiles getting stuck at an unconnected Condition node — unconnected branches cause silent journey exits.

questions.question: What is the key benefit of using Content Templates in AJO for marketing teams?
options: Templates are processed faster than custom emails, reducing delivery latency
Content-oriented users can work on templates independently; marketing users adapt them inside journeys/campaigns
Templates automatically translate email content into multiple languages based on profile locale
Using templates eliminates the need for HTML knowledge in the email designer
questions.explanation: AJO Content Templates enable a collaborative, parallel workflow: content designers and specialists can build and maintain templates outside of journeys or campaigns as standalone governed assets. Marketing users can then select, reuse, and customize these templates when building their journeys/campaigns — accelerating creation and ensuring brand consistency without duplication. This division of labor is especially valuable in larger organizations where brand and legal teams need to control certain content elements (like disclaimer text or visual identity) while campaign teams need freedom in campaign-specific sections. Templates also reduce time-to-market: instead of building an email from scratch, a marketer selects an approved template and fills in campaign-specific content in minutes rather than hours. From a governance perspective, templates can be locked to prevent modification of certain sections — a critical capability in regulated industries (financial services, pharma) where specific legal language must appear verbatim. Templates support all channel types: Email, Push, SMS, and In-App.

questions.question: In AJO, how are saved templates listed and sorted in the 'Saved templates' tab of the Email Designer?
options: By channel type, then alphabetically within each channel category
By name, Last modified date, and Last created date
By campaign performance metrics like open rate and click rate
By content type (promotional, transactional, operational)
questions.explanation: In the AJO Email Designer, the Saved Templates tab lists all content templates created for the current sandbox and allows sorting by three criteria: By name (alphabetical A-Z or Z-A), Last modified (most recently updated first — useful for finding a template you were just working on), and Last created (most recently created first — useful for finding brand new templates). This helps marketers quickly find templates by recency or name without scrolling through an extensive library. A practical tip: adopting a consistent naming convention like `[Brand]-[Channel]-[Purpose]-[Version]` (e.g., `AcmeCo-Email-WelcomeSeries-v3`) makes name-sort significantly more useful in large template libraries. Templates are sandbox-scoped — templates created in a development sandbox are not automatically available in production and must be exported/imported or recreated. The tab also supports thumbnail previews on hover, allowing marketers to visually identify the correct template without opening each one individually.

questions.question: What happens when you select 'Change your design' in the AJO Code Editor?
options: Opens the visual drag-and-drop designer while preserving existing HTML code
Clears the current email content and starts from a new, blank design
Switches from Code Editor to the Segment Builder for content personalization
Opens the template gallery to replace the current design with a saved template
questions.explanation: In the AJO Code Editor, selecting 'Change your design' from the options menu clears the current email content entirely and starts fresh from a new blank design. This is an effectively irreversible action within the current session — any custom HTML that was written is permanently discarded when confirmed. This option is useful when completely rethinking the email layout rather than iterating on the existing code — for example, if you initially started with a plain-text approach but need to switch to a fully designed HTML template. A practical warning: AJO does not always show a robust 'Are you sure?' confirmation dialog, so it is good practice to copy your HTML to an external editor before selecting this option as a safety measure. If you want to preserve your code while also switching to a template-based approach, the recommended workflow is to save your raw HTML externally first, then use 'Change your design' to start fresh, and manually migrate any reusable logic into the new structure.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'Preview' button in the AJO Email Designer?
options: To send a test email to up to 5 email addresses for visual verification
To check message design and personalization rendering using test profiles
To preview the email in multiple email clients (Gmail, Outlook, Apple Mail)
To generate a screenshot of the email for stakeholder approval workflows
questions.explanation: The Preview button in the AJO Email Designer allows you to check how the message design looks and how personalization expressions render using specific test profiles. You can select different test profiles to see how the email appears for different customer personas with their specific attribute values — for example, comparing the rendering for a Gold tier member vs. a Standard tier member. This is distinct from the 'Send test email' function (which actually delivers the email to a real inbox for email client rendering verification) and from 'Simulate' (which renders with randomized test profile data). The Preview renders the email in the AJO designer iframe — it does not guarantee pixel-perfect rendering across all email clients since Outlook, Gmail, and Apple Mail each have different HTML/CSS rendering engines. Best practice: use Preview first to validate personalization logic (verifying that `{{#if}}` conditions and `{{each}}` loops behave as expected), then use 'Send test email' to validate actual client rendering. Test profiles must be created in advance in AEP Profiles and should represent meaningful persona variations across your audience.

questions.question: In AJO, what are 'Custom Actions' primarily used for within journeys?
options: Creating custom audience segment rules that override default segmentation logic
Connecting AJO to third-party systems via REST API calls to send messages or trigger external logic
Defining custom merge policies that override the default AEP merge policy
Creating custom email templates using proprietary HTML frameworks
questions.explanation: Custom Actions in AJO are integration connectors that allow journeys to communicate with external systems (3rd-party CRMs, loyalty platforms, external APIs, notification services) via REST API calls. They extend AJO's capabilities beyond built-in channels — for example, triggering a CRM status update when a customer completes a purchase, calling a weather API to inject current conditions into an email, posting to a custom WhatsApp Business API, or updating a loyalty points balance in a downstream system. Custom Actions are configured once by an administrator (with endpoint URL, authentication, headers, and payload schema) and then become reusable nodes available to journey designers across all journeys in the sandbox. A key technical detail: Custom Action calls are made synchronously within the journey — the profile waits at the node until a response is received or a timeout occurs. The timeout is configurable (default 30 seconds); if exceeded, the journey follows the error path. Custom Actions should therefore not be used for slow external systems without caching or timeout handling in the target API.

questions.question: When configuring a Custom Action in AJO, what HTTP methods are supported for calling external endpoints?
options: Only POST for sending data to external systems
POST, PUT, and GET
GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE
POST and GET only, for security reasons
questions.explanation: AJO Custom Actions support three HTTP methods when calling external REST endpoints: POST (sending data to create records, trigger events, or send messages — the most commonly used method), PUT (updating existing records in full, like overwriting a customer's preference object in a CRM), and GET (retrieving data from an external source — useful when you need to fetch real-time data like current product availability or personalized recommendation scores). The exclusion of PATCH and DELETE is a deliberate safety constraint — AJO's design assumes external calls from journeys are predominantly data-submission or data-retrieval operations, not destructive modifications. In practice, many enterprise integrations use POST as the universal method even for updates, by passing an action type indicator in the payload body. When using GET requests, note that payload bodies are not supported per the HTTP specification — all parameters must be passed as query string parameters, which AJO supports via dynamic URL construction using profile attribute expressions.

questions.question: In AJO, what are 'Integration Points' in the context of Custom Actions?
options: The authentication settings (API keys, OAuth tokens) that connect AJO to external systems
The moments in a journey where AJO calls out to external systems via a Custom Action node
The data mapping configurations that transform AEP profile data into external system formats
The error handling fallback paths that activate when a Custom Action fails
questions.explanation: Integration Points are the specific nodes on the journey canvas where AJO calls external systems. Each Custom Action node placed on the canvas IS an integration point — when a profile reaches that node, AJO invokes the configured REST endpoint (URL, method, auth, headers, payload) to connect the journey to the downstream external system. A single journey can have multiple integration points — for example, one Custom Action to call a recommendation engine, followed by a Condition to branch on the response, followed by another Custom Action to log the outcome to a data warehouse. Understanding integration points as canvas nodes (rather than configuration settings) is important because each placement independently participates in Capping and Throttling rules — a Custom Action configured with a 100-calls/second cap applies that limit across ALL journey nodes that reference it, not per individual node placement. Two integration points in the same journey using the same Custom Action therefore share the same rate limit bucket.

questions.question: What is the difference between Capping and Throttling in AJO's rate limiting for external system calls?
options: Capping applies to email channels; Throttling applies to Custom Actions only
Capping discards calls that exceed the maximum rate; Throttling queues them for processing in order
Capping is a soft limit (degrades performance); Throttling is a hard limit (blocks calls)
Capping is per journey; Throttling is per sandbox across all journeys
questions.explanation: Capping: when the maximum configured rate is reached, EXCESS CALLS ARE DISCARDED (dropped) — the profile proceeds through the journey but the external call is never made. Throttling: when the maximum configured rate is reached, EXCESS CALLS ARE QUEUED and processed in order as capacity becomes available — no calls are lost, but latency increases for queued profiles. Capping is the more aggressive approach (prevents overload at the cost of dropped calls) and is suitable when the external system genuinely cannot handle more load and partial delivery is acceptable. Throttling is gentler (preserves all calls at the cost of increased latency) and is suitable when every call must eventually complete — for example, a loyalty points update that must not be silently skipped. A key design consideration: throttling introduces variable latency into the journey — a profile entering during a traffic spike may experience significantly longer waits at throttled Custom Action nodes than one entering during low traffic. Both mechanisms are configured in the AJO admin console and applied globally at the sandbox level for a specific endpoint URL.

questions.question: Where are Capping and Throttling rules configured in AJO, and what is their scope?
options: At the journey level; rules apply only to the specific journey they are configured in
At the sandbox level for a specific endpoint; rules apply globally to all journeys in that sandbox
At the organization level; rules apply across all sandboxes and journeys in the org
At the channel level; rules apply to all messages sent through a specific channel type
questions.explanation: Capping and Throttling rules are configured at the SANDBOX level for a specific endpoint URL. They are global within that sandbox — all journeys calling the same endpoint share the same rate limit. This ensures that even if 100 different journeys call the same external CRM endpoint simultaneously, the TOTAL rate across all of them is capped/throttled to protect the external system from overload. This sandbox-scoped, endpoint-keyed design has a critical architectural implication: if you accidentally configure two slightly different URLs pointing to the same external system (e.g., `https://api.crm.com/update` vs. `https://api.crm.com/update/v2`), they will have separate independent rate limit buckets — potentially allowing twice the intended load through. Always use consistent, canonical endpoint URLs and verify the exact URL match when configuring rules. Also note that rules configured in a development sandbox do NOT carry over to production — they must be recreated per sandbox.

questions.question: Which rate limiting technique is available for both Data Sources AND Custom Actions in AJO?
options: Throttling (queue-based rate limiting)
Capping (discard-based rate limiting)
Both Capping and Throttling are available for both
Neither - Data Sources have their own separate rate limiting mechanism
questions.explanation: In AJO, Capping is available for BOTH data sources and custom actions. Throttling is available ONLY for custom actions. This asymmetry exists because data sources (external data lookups used to enrich profiles during journey execution) are typically read-only, time-sensitive calls where a dropped call (Capping) is preferable to an indefinitely queued one — a profile enrichment that is capped can fall back to cached profile data, whereas a queued enrichment call would block the journey for an unpredictable duration. Custom Actions, by contrast, often represent writes or notifications where losing a call is more consequential, making queue-based Throttling more appropriate. A practical example: if your journey enriches profiles by calling a real-time pricing API (a data source) with a Capping rule of 800 calls/second, the excess calls are dropped and those profiles proceed using cached pricing. If the journey also calls a CRM update API (a custom action) with a Throttling rule of 800 calls/second, the excess calls are queued and will complete with a delay — ensuring every CRM update eventually lands.

questions.question: In AJO, what is the 'Data Exchange' process when a Custom Action calls an external REST API?
options: AJO syncs profile data from AEP to the external system's database before the journey runs
The runtime request/response flow where AJO sends a JSON payload and optionally maps the response back into the journey
A nightly batch export of journey engagement data to the external system for reporting
A bidirectional identity sync between AEP's Identity Graph and the external system's ID store
questions.explanation: Data Exchange in the Custom Action context is the runtime request/response cycle: AJO sends a JSON payload (built dynamically from journey context and profile data) to the external REST API, and optionally parses the JSON response to map specific fields back into the journey context for downstream use. These mapped response fields can then be used in Condition activities, subsequent action payloads, and personalization expressions within the same journey. For example: AJO calls a recommendation engine (sends `customerId` + `pageContext`), the engine responds with `{"recommendedProductId": "SKU-12345", "discount": 15}`, and AJO maps those fields into journey context variables used in the next email action's content. A technical detail: response mapping is configured in the Custom Action's 'Response Handling' section, where you define the JSON path for each field to extract. Only explicitly mapped fields are available for downstream use — AJO does not automatically expose the entire response payload. Response data is ephemeral (journey-session scoped) and is not written back to the permanent Real-Time Customer Profile unless a separate AEP Profile Update action is also configured.

questions.question: In AJO, what does a 'Custom Action node' do when a profile reaches it in a journey?
options: It applies a custom data governance policy check before proceeding
It invokes the configured REST endpoint sending a JSON payload built from journey context
It creates a custom profile attribute update in the Real-Time Customer Profile
It branches the profile to a custom path defined by a dynamic condition expression
questions.explanation: When a profile reaches a Custom Action node in a journey, AJO executes the REST call to the configured external endpoint. The JSON payload is built dynamically from journey context: profile attributes from the Real-Time Customer Profile, event data that triggered the journey, data retrieved by earlier data source lookups, and response fields from previous Custom Action calls in the same journey instance. The external system processes the request and returns a response, which AJO can optionally parse and map into journey-scoped variables for downstream use. If the external call fails (non-2xx HTTP response, network timeout, or DNS failure), AJO routes the profile to the error path of the Custom Action node — making proper error path design critical for journey resilience. Best practice: always connect the Custom Action's error path to either a meaningful fallback action (e.g., send a generic email instead of the personalized one) or an End node. AJO supports configuring a fixed number of retry attempts with a configurable delay between retries for transient failures, which should be used for any external system that may experience intermittent network issues.

questions.question: What is the AJO journey canvas step for using a custom action's response data in subsequent conditions?
options: The response data automatically merges into the profile for use in all downstream activities
A Condition activity after the Custom Action node uses 'Advanced Mode' to access payload response fields
The response data is stored in a global journey variable accessible by all subsequent activities
A 'Response Mapper' activity must be added after every Custom Action to make data available
questions.explanation: After a Custom Action node, to use response data in a Condition you add a Condition activity and switch it to 'Advanced Mode' within the condition editor. In Advanced Mode, the Context data menu shows all available data sources for the journey — including the response payload fields mapped in the Custom Action's Response Handling configuration. From there you can construct expressions like `#{WeatherCustomAction.response.temperature} > 30` to branch profiles based on the API response value. A common gotcha: response fields are only accessible in Advanced Mode — the simplified condition builder does not expose Custom Action response data. Response data is journey-session scoped: it is available to all downstream activities in that profile's journey instance but is not persisted to the AEP Real-Time Customer Profile. For example, using a temperature value returned by a weather API to route Gold-tier members in cities above 30°C to a summer promotion path, while others receive the standard path — each field in the response must have been explicitly mapped during Custom Action configuration to appear in the Advanced Mode context menu.

questions.question: Which two implementation modes are supported for AJO Web Channel to enable authoring and delivery?
options: Server-side only and client-side only
Client-side only (Web SDK) and Hybrid mode (Server API + Web SDK rendering)
Native app SDK mode and Progressive Web App mode
Adobe Target mode and Adobe Journey Optimizer native mode
questions.explanation: AJO Web Channel supports two implementation modes: Client-side only (Web SDK/alloy.js handles both the personalization decision request to the Edge Network and the subsequent DOM rendering of modifications — simplest to implement, works well for SPAs) and Hybrid mode (AEP Edge Network Server API requests personalization server-side during page generation, while alloy.js renders the returned proposition JSON client-side — better for SSR frameworks and SEO-sensitive content). Both modes require alloy.js on the client for actual content rendering because the visual modifications authored in the AJO web designer are expressed as DOM manipulation instructions that must execute in the browser. The client-side only mode is recommended for teams new to AJO web personalization as it requires only alloy.js configuration with `renderDecisions: true`. Hybrid mode requires additional server-side infrastructure: the web application server makes an authenticated Server API call, parses the response propositions, embeds them in the HTML payload, and alloy.js applies the pre-fetched propositions rather than fetching them again. Hybrid mode is preferred for performance-conscious implementations because it eliminates the client-side Edge Network round-trip and reduces the risk of a visible flash of unmodified content (FOUC).

questions.question: What does the 'If/Unless' Helper in AJO's personalization expression editor enable?
options: Creating A/B test variants directly within the email template markup
Defining conditional blocks that show different content based on evaluated conditions
Applying transformation functions to profile attributes before rendering them
Looping through arrays of items to render dynamic product lists in emails
questions.explanation: The If/Unless Helper in AJO enables conditional content blocks within messages. `{{#if condition}}...{{else}}...{{/if}}` renders different content based on whether the condition evaluates to true; `{{#unless condition}}...{{/unless}}` is the logical inverse — it renders when the condition is false. This is fundamental for dynamic personalization: showing different offers to Gold vs. Standard loyalty members, displaying locale-appropriate legal text, or greeting returning customers differently. Conditions can reference profile attributes, contextual data, and helper function results: `{{#if profile.loyaltyTier == 'Gold'}}Exclusive offer just for you{{else}}Check out our deals{{/if}}`. Nested If blocks are supported for multi-level branching, though deeply nested logic can become hard to maintain — consider extracting complex conditions into named expression fragments for readability. A practical gotcha: `{{#if}}` evaluates JavaScript-style truthiness — empty strings, `0`, `null`, and `undefined` are all falsy. This means `{{#if profile.loyaltyPoints}}` renders the else block when points equal exactly 0, which may not be the intended behavior — use an explicit comparison like `{{#if profile.loyaltyPoints > 0}}` to be safe.

questions.question: What does the 'Each' Helper in AJO's personalization template language do?
options: Iterates through arrays to render dynamic content for each item in the array
Applies a function to each character of a string value
Repeats the same content block a specified number of times
Evaluates each condition in a switch-case block independently
questions.explanation: The `Each` Helper in AJO templates iterates through arrays, rendering a content block for each item in the array. For example, `{{#each purchasedItems}}...{{name}} - {{price}}...{{/each}}` loops through all items in a customer's recent order and renders a product row for each one — enabling dynamic order confirmation emails, personalized product recommendation sections, and cart abandonment emails that list every abandoned item by name. Inside the `each` block, you access the current item's properties directly without the array path prefix. AJO also provides `{{@index}}` (the zero-based index of the current iteration) and `{{@first}}`/`{{@last}}` boolean helpers to conditionally render content only for the first or last item — useful for adding separators between items or a 'View all' link only at the end. A practical consideration: the `each` block renders for every array item, so if `purchasedItems` contains 50 items, 50 product blocks are rendered. Always pair `each` with a length check or a `limit` slice helper to prevent unexpectedly long emails for customers with large order or browsing histories.

questions.question: What is the 'Let' Helper used for in AJO's personalization expressions?
options: Declaring profile attributes as locally scoped variables to avoid namespace conflicts
Storing and reusing complex expressions as named variables within a template
Setting default values for profile attributes that may be undefined
Permitting access to restricted profile fields in compliance with governance policies
questions.explanation: The `Let` Helper allows storing a complex or frequently referenced expression as a named variable that can be used multiple times throughout the template without repeating the full expression. Instead of repeating `{{profile.person.name.firstName}}` in every salutation, heading, and body paragraph, you assign it once: `{{let firstName = profile.person.name.firstName}}` and reference `{{firstName}}` everywhere. This makes templates more readable, maintainable, and less prone to copy-paste errors when the underlying data path changes — you update the `let` declaration once and all references automatically reflect the change. `Let` variables are scoped to the template block where they are declared: a `let` at the root of the template is available everywhere, while one declared inside a `{{#each}}` block is scoped to that iteration. A practical use case: computing a derived value once and reusing it — `{{let discountedPrice = product.price * 0.85}}` and then referencing `{{discountedPrice}}` in both the email body and the CTA button label. Without `Let`, changing the discount calculation from 15% to 20% would require finding and updating every occurrence of the inline expression.

questions.question: What does the 'With' Helper do in AJO's personalization template language?
options: Combines multiple profile attributes into a single formatted string value
Modifies the evaluation context of a template block to access specific nested data more easily
Applies mathematical transformations to numerical profile attributes
Grants temporary access to restricted data fields within a governance-controlled block
questions.explanation: The `With` Helper modifies the evaluation context within a template block, making it easier to access deeply nested data by setting a new 'root' context for that block. For example, `{{#with profile.person.name}}...{{firstName}} {{lastName}}...{{/with}}` sets the context to `profile.person.name`, so you reference `firstName` and `lastName` directly without the full path prefix. This is especially useful when working with deeply nested XDM schema objects where the full path becomes verbose: `profile.homeAddress.street1` becomes simply `street1` inside a `{{#with profile.homeAddress}}` block. You can also use `With` with event data or context objects: `{{#with context.journey.order}}...Order #{{orderId}}, Total: {{orderTotal}}...{{/with}}`. Inside a `With` block, you can still access the parent context using the `../` prefix (`{{../profile.loyaltyTier}}`) when you need data outside the scoped context. `With` and `Each` can be nested together — for example, `{{#with order}}{{#each items}}...{{/each}}{{/with}}` to iterate over order items within an order context, keeping expressions readable and concise.

questions.question: In AJO, what is the 'Default Fallback Value' Helper used for?
options: Specifying a backup journey path when a Custom Action call fails
Rendering a default value when a profile variable is empty or undefined
Providing a fallback offer when no eligible offer is found in the offer library
Defining a default merge policy when the active merge policy has no matching data
questions.explanation: The Default Fallback Value Helper in AJO renders a specified default value when a profile variable is empty, null, or undefined. The syntax is `{{profile.person.name.firstName | default: 'Valued Customer'}}` — if `firstName` is missing from the profile, the email renders 'Valued Customer' instead of a blank space or a broken layout. This is essential for preventing broken personalization in production emails where not all profiles have all attributes populated. Common use cases include fallback first names (`| default: 'Friend'`), fallback city names for location-based content (`| default: 'your area'`), and fallback product names for recommendation blocks. A practical note: the fallback value is a static string — it does not support nested expressions like `| default: profile.preferredName`. If you need dynamic fallback logic (try firstName, fall back to username, then to 'Valued Customer'), use nested `{{#if}}` blocks instead. The Default helper is also invaluable during template testing: by providing meaningful fallbacks, you can preview email rendering for test profiles that have sparse data without the rendering failing entirely due to undefined field references.

questions.question: What is the role of 'Web Content Experimentation' in AJO?
options: Testing different audience segment definitions to find the most effective targeting rules
Running experiments with different content strategies on web channels to identify the most effective approach
A/B testing different journey paths to optimize the overall customer flow
Experimenting with different merge policies to improve profile completeness
questions.explanation: Web Content Experimentation in AJO allows businesses to run controlled content experiments on their web properties — testing different versions of content, layouts, offers, CTAs, or messaging to identify which approach drives the best engagement and conversion metrics. Results inform which content strategy to scale for the full audience. Technically, AJO web experimentation works by randomly assigning visitors to different treatment groups (variants) using a deterministic hash of the visitor's ECID, ensuring consistent variant assignment on repeat visits to the same experiment. Each variant is authored as a separate web channel experience in the AJO interface, and the traffic split percentage per variant is configurable. Experiment results are measured via KPIs defined at setup time (e.g., link click rate, custom conversion events sent to AEP via alloy.js `sendEvent`). A key best practice: define your primary KPI and minimum detectable effect before starting the experiment, and use AJO's built-in statistical confidence visualizations to determine when to declare a winner. Stopping experiments too early ('peeking') is a common mistake that leads to false positives.

questions.question: In AJO, what is the purpose of the 'Condition' step in a journey that follows a Custom Action?
options: To validate that the Custom Action completed without errors before proceeding
To use Custom Action response data to determine which journey path each profile takes
To apply data governance policies based on the external system's response
To retry the Custom Action call if the initial response was a timeout or error
questions.explanation: After a Custom Action, a Condition activity uses the response data returned by the external API to make routing decisions that personalize the remainder of the journey. For example: after calling a weather API (Custom Action), a Condition checks `temperature > 30°C` and routes profiles in hot-weather cities to a 'summer product' email path while others receive a 'standard offer' email. This Custom Action + Condition pattern is the fundamental building block for data-driven, real-time adaptive journeys in AJO. To access Custom Action response fields in a Condition, switch the editor to 'Advanced Mode' and use the Context data picker to navigate to the mapped response fields. A journey design best practice: place the Condition immediately after the Custom Action (with no Wait or other activity in between) to use the freshest possible response data. If a Wait node is placed between the Custom Action and the Condition, response data is still available (it persists in the journey instance session), but ensure the external data has not become stale during the wait period. Always handle cases where the Custom Action returns unexpected or missing fields — the Condition's 'Otherwise' branch should route these edge-case profiles gracefully rather than leaving them stranded.

questions.question: What is AJO's Expression Editor primarily used for in the context of content fragments?
options: Building PQL-based segment rules for audience-based journey entries
Saving personalization expressions as reusable 'expression fragments' for use in content
Editing the JSON schema structure of profile attributes used in personalization
Creating dynamic conditions that control when a journey branch is executed
questions.explanation: AJO's Expression Editor allows saving personalization expressions (like complex date calculations, conditional loyalty tier lookups, or reusable data transformations) as named 'expression fragments.' These saved expressions are then available in the Expression Editor's fragment library for use across multiple messages — enabling complex logic to be written once by a specialist and reused by all marketers without them needing to understand the underlying syntax. For example, a complex expression that formats a date relative to a customer's loyalty anniversary and adjusts for their timezone could be saved as an expression fragment named `loyaltyAnniversaryGreeting` and referenced in 50 different campaigns. Expression fragments support all standard AJO helper functions and operators. A key governance benefit: if the underlying data schema changes (e.g., a profile attribute is renamed), you update the expression fragment once and all messages using it automatically reflect the fix on their next send — without hunting through dozens of individual email templates. Expression fragments can also be reviewed by a data governance team before being made available to the broader marketing team, adding a quality gate to personalization logic.

questions.question: In AJO, what is the 'Real-time Engagement' capability of the Web Channel?
options: Delivering web push notifications to users who have given browser notification permission
Providing personalized content based on dynamically updated customer profiles in real-time
Enabling real-time chat functionality within web experiences delivered by AJO
Processing web analytics events in real-time to update audience membership instantly
questions.explanation: The AJO Web Channel's Real-Time Engagement capability allows delivery of personalized content based on the dynamically updated Real-Time Customer Profile. When a user visits the website, AJO (via the Edge Network) evaluates their current profile state — including recent behavioral signals processed through streaming ingestion (purchases made minutes ago, products viewed in the current session, loyalty tier changes applied moments earlier) — to deliver contextually relevant, individualized experiences without any batch processing delay. This is what distinguishes AJO web personalization from traditional batch-segmentation-based approaches: a customer who upgrades their loyalty tier during one session can see Gold-member experiences on the same visit if streaming segmentation has processed the event. The profile evaluation happens at the Edge Network geographically close to the user for low-latency responses. A practical consideration: the 'real-time' nature depends on how quickly the AEP streaming pipeline processes behavioral events — high-volume bursts can introduce seconds to minutes of lag, so extremely time-sensitive personalization (like reacting to a cart addition within the same page load) may need to be supplemented with client-side state management passed directly to alloy.js `sendEvent` calls.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a Journey and a Campaign in Adobe Journey Optimizer?
options: Journeys support all channels; Campaigns only support Email and SMS channels
Journeys orchestrate individual, multi-step experiences triggered by events or schedules; Campaigns deliver one-time or recurring messages to audiences
Journeys are for known profiles only; Campaigns support both anonymous and known profiles
Journeys are created by admins; Campaigns are created by marketing users
questions.explanation: In AJO, Journeys are multi-step orchestration flows that guide individual profiles through personalized, stateful experiences triggered by events or on a schedule — with branching (Condition), pacing (Wait), external integration (Custom Actions), and multi-channel touchpoints woven into a single automated workflow. Campaigns are broadcast communications — one-time or recurring messages sent to an entire audience segment at a specific scheduled time without multi-step logic. A concrete example: a post-purchase journey (Journey) might send an order confirmation email, wait 3 days, check if the product was delivered, send a satisfaction survey, wait 7 days, and then send a complementary product recommendation — all personalized to the individual's specific order details. A summer sale announcement (Campaign) simply sends one email to everyone in the 'Active Customers' segment on a chosen date at 10am. From an authoring perspective, Journeys are built in the Journey Canvas (a node-based visual workflow editor), while Campaigns use a simpler form-based configuration approach. Both share the same channel delivery infrastructure, channel surface configuration, and Email Designer tooling.

questions.question: In AJO, when using the Code Editor for email, what prefix is used to reference profile attributes in personalization?
options: customer.*
user.*
profile.*
contact.*
questions.explanation: In AJO's email Code Editor and expression engine, Real-Time Customer Profile attributes are referenced using the `profile.*` prefix. For example: `{{profile.person.name.firstName}}` for first name, `{{profile.homeAddress.city}}` for city, `{{profile.loyaltyDetails.tier}}` for a loyalty tier field. This consistent prefix distinguishes profile data from contextual data (`context.*`, which includes journey-level variables and event data), offer data (`offers.*`), and helper function outputs. The `profile.*` path follows the XDM schema structure of the customer's merged profile — so the exact path depends on how your XDM schema is structured in AEP. A frequent mistake for new AJO users is trying to reference custom profile attributes using a flat name like `{{profile.myCustomAttribute}}` when the attribute is actually nested under a custom mixin: `{{profile._tenantId.myCustomMixin.myCustomAttribute}}`. The tenant namespace prefix (`_tenantId`) is required for all custom non-standard XDM fields. Use the Expression Editor's schema browser (which shows the actual schema tree) rather than guessing paths — incorrect paths silently render as empty strings in delivered emails.

questions.question: What AJO feature enables marketers to design visual web page modifications without needing to write code?
options: The AJO Code Editor with HTML templates
The Visual Interface / Web Designer that provides WYSIWYG editing capabilities
The Segment Builder with visual audience targeting tools
The Journey Canvas with drag-and-drop content blocks
questions.explanation: The AJO Web Channel includes a visual interface (Web Designer) that provides a WYSIWYG (What You See Is What You Get) editing experience. Marketers can directly manipulate web page elements — text, images, buttons, containers, and layouts — without writing code, by clicking and editing elements directly on a live preview of the web page loaded inside the AJO designer interface. The Web Designer supports actions like Replace (swap an image or text block), Insert Before/After (add new content adjacent to an element), Move (reorder elements on the page), Hide (suppress an element from view for specific audiences), and Delete (remove an element entirely). Each modification is recorded as a structured action that alloy.js replays at runtime when delivering the personalized experience to site visitors. An important practical warning: the Web Designer loads the actual live website inside an iframe, so changes deployed to the website's underlying code (HTML/CSS/JS) after an AJO web experience is authored can break the element selectors used by AJO modifications — a common cause of web personalization breaking after a site redesign. Using stable semantic selectors (IDs or dedicated data attributes) rather than fragile positional CSS selectors mitigates this risk.

questions.question: In AJO Custom Action configuration, what does the 'Required Marketing Action' field define?
options: The mandatory action type (email, push, SMS) that must be linked to the custom action
The data governance marketing action associated with this custom action for policy enforcement
The business rule that determines when the custom action is triggered in a journey
The SLA requirement (response time) that the external system must meet
questions.explanation: The 'Required Marketing Action' in Custom Action configuration links the action to AEP Data Governance policies. Marketing actions (such as 'Email Marketing,' 'Analytics,' 'Data Science,' 'Third-party sharing,' or custom-defined actions) are defined in AEP and associated with data usage policies that specify which data labels (C1 through C12) are restricted for which marketing actions. When a Custom Action is used in a journey, AJO evaluates whether any profile data fields used in the action's payload carry data usage labels that conflict with the assigned marketing action's policies. If a conflict exists, AJO blocks the journey from publishing with a governance violation error identifying the exact field and conflicting policy. A concrete example: if your Custom Action shares data with a third-party analytics vendor and carries the 'Analytics' marketing action, and your governance policy states that C2-labeled data (Export Data Controlled) cannot be used for Analytics, then the journey will fail to publish if any C2-labeled field appears in the action's payload. This makes governance enforcement automatic and preventative rather than retroactive, protecting both customer data and regulatory compliance.

questions.question: In AJO's journey canvas, what is the function of a 'Read Segment' entry node?
options: It reads the segment definition rules and generates the audience in real-time
It enters all profiles currently qualifying for a segment into the journey at a scheduled time
It reads profile data from an external segment stored in a third-party CDP
It continuously monitors segment membership and enters new qualifiers as they are added
questions.explanation: The 'Read Segment' entry node (audience-based journey) enters all profiles that currently qualify for a specified segment into the journey at a scheduled execution time, processing them in bulk. Unlike event-triggered entries (unitary journeys that enter profiles one at a time in real-time), Read Segment journeys are executed as a scheduled batch — all qualifying profiles enter at the configured run time, making them suitable for planned campaigns and scheduled communications like monthly newsletters, seasonal promotions, or quarterly loyalty tier review communications. Read Segment journeys support both one-time execution and recurring schedules (daily, weekly, monthly). For recurring journeys, each execution creates a new 'journey run,' and you can configure re-entry rules — whether a profile who entered in the previous run can re-enter the next run, or only net-new segment members enter. The segment evaluated is the most recent batch evaluation snapshot — meaning streaming segment updates that occur between batch runs are not reflected until the next batch evaluation completes. For time-sensitive audience targeting, pair a batch-evaluated Read Segment entry with a streaming-evaluated segment definition to minimize the gap between qualification and journey entry.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'Publish' action for a journey in AJO?
options: Saves the journey as a draft for future editing without making it live
Makes the journey live and active - new profiles can now enter the journey
Shares the journey configuration with other sandbox environments
Publishes a performance report of the journey to the reporting dashboard
questions.explanation: Publishing a journey in AJO activates it — making it live and allowing profiles to enter and progress through it according to the configured entry conditions (event triggers or schedule). Before publishing, a journey is in Draft mode and no profiles can enter, making Draft safe for iterative design and review. Publishing is a critical milestone that triggers several automatic validation checks: data governance policy validation (checking that profile data used in custom actions complies with assigned marketing actions), channel surface validation (verifying that email/push/SMS surfaces referenced are active and correctly configured), and schema validation (ensuring all referenced event and profile fields exist in the current schema). If any check fails, publishing is blocked with descriptive error messages. Once published, the journey cannot be edited in-place — to make changes you must create a new version (via 'Create a new version') while the current version remains live for profiles already in it. New profiles then enter the updated version. A practical tip: use journey duplication for major redesigns to preserve the original as a reference while building the revised version independently.

questions.question: What helper function categories are available in the AJO Expression Editor?
options: Text, Number, Date, Boolean, and Array functions
Aggregation/Array, Date Time, Map, Math, Object, and String functions; plus Helpers like Each/If/Let/With
Format, Transform, Validate, Aggregate, and Compare functions
Profile, Event, Context, Segment, and Journey data access functions
questions.explanation: AJO Expression Editor provides a comprehensive set of capabilities across three categories. Functions include: Aggregation/Array (count, sum, distinct, first, last, flatten — for working with arrays of experience events or purchased items), Date Time (formatDate, toDateTimeOnly, addDays, dayOfWeek — for date formatting and arithmetic), Map (getAndApply — for key-value map lookups), Math (round, floor, ceil, abs, min, max — for numerical calculations), Object (isNotNull, isNull — for null-checking), and String (concat, upper, lower, trim, length, replace, startWith, endWith, matches — for text manipulation). Helpers include: Default Fallback Value, Each (array iteration), If/Unless (conditional blocks), Let (variable assignment), and With (context scoping). Operators include: Arithmetic (+, -, *, /), Boolean (and, or, not), and Comparison (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=). A data-savvy marketer can construct expressions like `{{formatDate(addDays(profile.loyaltyEnrollmentDate, 365), 'MMMM d, yyyy')}}` to display a loyalty anniversary date in a human-readable format entirely within the AJO interface — no code deployment required. This comprehensive toolkit makes complex personalization logic accessible to non-developers while keeping it auditable and reusable.

questions.question: In AJO, what is the 'Offer Library' and what does it contain?
options: A collection of pre-built email templates categorized by industry vertical
A central catalog containing offers with content, eligibility rules, and delivery constraints
A repository of approved brand assets including images, logos, and color palettes
A library of pre-written subject line variations for A/B testing email campaigns
questions.explanation: The AJO Offer Library is the central catalog for all offers used in Offer Decisioning (also called Experience Decisioning). Each offer in the library contains three core components: Content (the visual and text elements — images, headlines, body copy, CTAs — configured per channel representation), Eligibility Rules (the audience criteria determining which profiles qualify — referencing segment membership, profile attributes, or event history), and Constraints (limits on how many times the offer can be shown globally across all customers, or per individual customer, and the active date range). The Offer Library also organizes offers into Collections (curated groups used as the candidate pool for a decision) and supports a Fallback Offer — a generic offer shown when no personalized offers match a given profile, ensuring every customer always sees something relevant. A key distinction: offers in the Offer Library are separate from email messages — an offer is a content + eligibility unit that can be embedded into an email, a web page, a push notification, or surfaced via API. The Offer Decision Engine selects the best matching offer from the library at runtime for each individual profile, rather than the marketer pre-assigning specific offers to specific audience segments.

questions.question: What is an 'Offer Placement' in AJO's Offer Decisioning?
options: A geographical region where an offer can be displayed based on customer location
A combination of a channel type and a specific location within that channel where an offer appears
The scheduling configuration that determines when an offer is active and eligible for delivery
The priority rank assigned to an offer within a specific decision container
questions.explanation: An Offer Placement defines WHERE an offer will be displayed — a combination of a channel type (email, mobile app, web, kiosk, or custom) and a specific named location within that channel (e.g., 'Hero Banner,' 'Email Top Section,' 'Homepage Product Slot,' 'Checkout Recommendation'). Placements are created by administrators and act as containers that define the expected content format (JSON, HTML, image URL, or plain text) for that location. Since one offer can appear in multiple placements, a single 'Summer Sale 20% Off' offer can have both an 'Email Hero Banner' representation (with a wide landscape image and email-appropriate HTML) and a 'Mobile App Interstitial' representation (with a portrait image and mobile-optimized copy) — the same business offer, delivered appropriately for each context. When building an Offer Decision, you scope it to a specific placement so that only offers with a matching representation are candidates for that decision. A practical gotcha: if an offer does not have a representation configured for the placement used in a decision, it will never be selected for that placement even if the profile is fully eligible — always verify that your offers have representations for every placement you intend to use them in.

questions.question: In AJO journeys, what is the difference between an 'Event' entry and a 'Read Segment' entry?
options: Event entries support all channels; Read Segment entries only support email
Event entries trigger for individual profiles in real-time on specific actions; Read Segment entries bulk-enter all segment members at a scheduled time
Event entries use streaming segmentation; Read Segment entries use batch segmentation
Event entries are for B2B journeys; Read Segment entries are for B2C journeys
questions.explanation: Event-triggered entries (Unitary journeys) enter one profile at a time in real-time when that specific profile triggers the configured event — for example, a `commerce.purchases` Experience Event is streamed to AEP and AJO reacts within seconds to enter the purchasing profile into a post-purchase journey. Read Segment entries (Audience-based journeys) bulk-enter ALL profiles currently in a segment at a scheduled time — more like a campaign blast to an entire audience cohort at a marketer-controlled moment. The real-time vs. batch distinction has major implications for use case design: event-triggered journeys are ideal for reactive, time-sensitive scenarios (welcome series, cart abandonment, password resets) where the communication's value is tied to immediacy. Read Segment journeys are ideal for planned, audience-wide communications (monthly statements, seasonal promotions, product launch announcements) where timing is marketer-controlled. A hybrid approach is also available via 'Segment Qualification' event entry — this enters profiles in real-time as they qualify for or exit a specific segment, combining the immediacy of event-triggered entry with audience-driven qualification conditions, and is particularly useful for loyalty tier change notifications or subscription expiry alerts.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'Representation' configuration when creating an offer in AJO?
options: Defines how the offer is visually represented in the Offer Library admin interface
Configures the channel and placement where the offer content will be delivered
Sets the ranking priority of the offer relative to other eligible offers
Defines the eligibility rules and audience criteria for the offer
questions.explanation: An offer Representation is the combination of a channel, a placement within that channel, and the actual content to be rendered in that placement — it defines WHERE and HOW the offer's content is delivered to a specific surface. One offer can have multiple representations: for example, a 'Summer Sale 20% Off' offer might have an 'Email Top Banner' representation (wide landscape JPEG + HTML wrapper), a 'Mobile App Push' representation (short title + body text), and a 'Web Homepage Hero' representation (CSS modifications + different copy length). This multi-representation design allows the same business offer to be delivered appropriately formatted for each touchpoint without duplicating the offer's eligibility rules and capping constraints — the offer entity is shared while the presentation layer is channel-specific. When configuring a Representation, you select the placement (which determines the expected content format) and then provide content in the matching format: JSON for web API deliveries, HTML for email, or image URLs for display placements. The Offer Decision Engine only considers offers that have a representation matching the requested placement — offers without a matching representation are invisible to that decision.

questions.question: What is the role of the 'Offer Decision Engine' in AJO Offer Decisioning?
options: Validates that all offers in the library comply with data governance policies
Picks the best offer to deliver to each profile based on eligibility, priority, and capping rules
Processes offer click and view events to update offer performance reports
Manages the offer lifecycle from draft through to expiration and archival
questions.explanation: The Offer Decision Engine is the core intelligence in AJO's Offer Decisioning. When a decision is triggered (via a journey action, a campaign, or a direct API call to the Decisioning API), the engine evaluates all offers in the configured decision's offer collection against each individual profile: first checking eligibility rules (does this profile qualify for this offer?), then checking capping rules (has this offer already been shown to this profile the maximum allowed number of times?), then ranking the remaining eligible, uncapped offers by their configured priority number or AI-generated ranking score, and finally selecting the single 'best offer' (or N best offers if the decision requests multiple slots) to deliver. This personalized selection happens dynamically in real-time for each individual — 10,000 customers requesting the same decision simultaneously each receive their individually optimized offer. A key distinction: the Decision Engine operates server-side at the Edge Network — it never exposes the full offer catalog to the client, only the winning offer's rendered content. This protects business-sensitive offer strategies (pricing tiers, eligibility thresholds) from client-side inspection and manipulation.

questions.question: In AJO, what does 'Capping' achieve in the context of offer management?
options: It limits the maximum duration an offer can remain active in the Offer Library
It sets the maximum number of times an offer can be presented to prevent over-solicitation
It caps the maximum discount percentage that can be applied to any single offer
It restricts the number of users who can create and manage offers in the Offer Library
questions.explanation: In AJO Offer Management, Capping defines the maximum number of times an offer can be presented — either in total across all customers (global cap) or per individual customer (per-profile cap). This prevents over-solicitation (showing the same offer so frequently that it loses perceived value or annoys the customer), protects promotional budgets (a 50%-off flash offer with a global cap of 1,000 stops being shown once 1,000 customers have seen it), and avoids offer fatigue. There are two capping dimensions configurable independently: Global Capping (total impressions across all recipients — e.g., this limited promotion can only be shown 5,000 times across the entire customer base) and Per-Profile Capping (maximum times shown to a single individual — e.g., show this offer to each customer at most 3 times before suppressing it). Once either cap is reached, the offer is automatically excluded from decisions — the next-best eligible offer or the fallback offer is shown instead. A practical note: capping counters are maintained at the Edge Network level and updated after each impression event; in very high-concurrency scenarios there can be slight over-counting due to race conditions, so design your caps with a small buffer if exact limits are business-critical.

questions.question: In AJO, what is 'Offer Priority' used for?
options: Setting the order in which offers are listed in the Offer Library browsing interface
Determining which offer is shown when a customer qualifies for more than one offer simultaneously
Controlling the sequence in which offers are activated during campaign scheduling
Ranking offers by expected revenue contribution for budget allocation purposes
questions.explanation: Offer Priority in AJO resolves conflicts when a customer qualifies for multiple eligible, uncapped offers at the same time within the same decision. The offer with the highest priority number wins and is presented to the customer — for example, a Gold Loyalty Member exclusive offer (priority 90) will always surface over a generic promotional offer (priority 10) when a Gold member qualifies for both. Priority values are integers configured per offer in the Offer Library, enabling marketers to encode business rules about offer precedence without building complex exclusion logic in eligibility rules. Beyond simple numeric priority, AJO also supports AI-powered ranking via 'Ranking Formulas' (custom mathematical expressions that calculate an offer's score based on profile attributes and offer metadata) and 'Auto-optimization' (an AI model that dynamically ranks offers based on predicted conversion likelihood for each individual profile, updated continuously as interaction data flows in). When AI ranking is enabled, the numeric priority serves as a tiebreaker or as the fallback ranking method for offers outside the AI model's scope. A practical design tip: reserve the highest priority ranges (80-100) for time-sensitive or high-value business offers (flash sales, one-time courtesy gestures) to ensure they always surface above routine promotional content regardless of AI model outputs.
